metadata.subject: Science
metadata.description: Geology 12 is an advanced senior course investigating the materials, history, and dynamic processes of Earth through the lens of geological science. Students examine mineral and rock formation, interpret geological history from strata and fossils, apply plate tectonic theory to understand Earth's structural changes, analyse rock deformation, and explore weathering and erosion. BC's rich geological heritage — including First Peoples knowledge of landscapes — is integrated throughout. The course prepares students for post-secondary earth sciences and resource industry pathways.
metadata.general_description: An advanced senior geology course examining earth materials, geological history, plate tectonics, rock deformation, and weathering and erosion — developing field and analytical skills foundational to earth sciences and resource management careers.

big_ideas.statement: Minerals, rocks, and earth materials form in response to conditions within and on the Earth's surface and are the foundation of many resource-based industries.
big_ideas.elaboration: The physical and chemical conditions under which minerals crystallize and rocks form determine their properties, distribution, and economic value — from gemstones and industrial minerals to fossil fuels and building materials.

big_ideas.statement: Earth's geological and biological history is interpreted and inferred from information stored in rock strata and fossil evidence.
big_ideas.elaboration: The rock record is Earth's archive — from the Hadean eon to the present. Stratigraphy, radiometric dating, and palaeontology allow geologists to reconstruct the history of Earth's surface, oceans, atmosphere, and life over billions of years.

big_ideas.statement: The plate tectonic theory explains the changes that occur within Earth and to Earth's crust throughout geological time.
big_ideas.elaboration: Plate tectonics provides a unified explanation for the formation of mountains, ocean basins, volcanoes, and earthquakes — and for the changing configuration of continents over geological time.

big_ideas.statement: The form, arrangement, and structure of rocks are affected by three-dimensional forces over time.
big_ideas.elaboration: Stress and strain applied to rocks over geological time produce folds, faults, joints, and cleavage — structures that record the tectonic history of a region and are critical to resource exploration and hazard assessment.

big_ideas.statement: Weathering and erosion processes continually reshape landscapes through the interaction of the geosphere with the hydrosphere and atmosphere.
big_ideas.elaboration: The breakdown of rock by physical and chemical weathering, followed by transport and deposition by water, wind, ice, and gravity, continuously reshapes Earth's surface — connecting geology to climate, ecosystems, and human settlements.

competencies.raw: Sustain intellectual curiosity; make observations to identify questions; formulate multiple hypotheses.
i_can_statements: I can formulate complex geological questions about earth materials, geological history, and dynamic Earth processes.
I can make careful field and laboratory observations and generate testable geological hypotheses.
I can predict geological outcomes based on knowledge of mineral chemistry, tectonic forces, and weathering processes.

competencies.raw: Plan and select investigation methods; assess risks; use SI units and digital technologies; apply accuracy and precision concepts.
i_can_statements: I can plan geological investigations — field mapping, sample collection, and laboratory analysis.
I can assess safety and ethical considerations in field and laboratory geology work.
I can use appropriate tools: hand lens, streak plate, acid test, compass, and digital mapping technologies.
I can apply accuracy and precision in geological measurements and geologic time calculations.

categories.name: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information
categories.raw: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information

competencies.raw: Classify and interpret data; apply First Peoples perspectives; analyze patterns and relationships; construct and interpret models.
i_can_statements: I can apply First Peoples knowledge of landscapes, landforms, and geological events to enrich geological analysis.
I can classify and interpret mineral and rock samples using physical and chemical properties.
I can construct and interpret geological cross-sections, maps, and stratigraphic columns.
I can analyze patterns in geological data including seismic records, radiometric dating results, and tectonic maps.

competencies.raw: Evaluate methods and identify errors; improve investigation quality; assess validity of models; consider ethical implications.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate the methods and limitations of geological dating techniques and stratigraphic interpretation.
I can assess the validity of geological models including plate tectonic reconstructions.
I can consider the ethical, environmental, and Indigenous rights implications of resource extraction in BC.

competencies.raw: Contribute to community solutions; design collaborative projects; implement multiple problem-solving strategies.
i_can_statements: I can apply geological knowledge to real-world problems including mineral exploration, hazard assessment, and environmental management.
I can connect geology learning to resource industry careers and geoscience pathways.
I can consider how geological knowledge supports sustainable resource management and Indigenous land stewardship.

competencies.raw: Formulate theoretical models; communicate scientific ideas with evidence; express perspectives through place-based connections.
i_can_statements: I can communicate geological interpretations using accurate terminology, maps, and cross-sections.
I can present evidence-based geological arguments about the history and structure of a region.
I can express how BC's geological landscape — and First Peoples relationships to it — shape my understanding of geology.

areas.name: Minerals and Rocks
areas.raw: Minerals, rocks, and earth materials forming in response to Earth conditions.
topics: Mineral identification: hardness (Mohs scale), cleavage, fracture, lustre, streak, specific gravity
Silicate minerals: structure, groups (feldspars, pyroxenes, micas, quartz), and common examples
Non-silicate minerals: oxides, sulfides, carbonates, native elements
Igneous rocks: composition (felsic to ultramafic), texture (phaneritic, aphanitic, glassy, vesicular), and formation
Sedimentary rocks: clastic (grain size classification), chemical, and biochemical; sedimentary environments
Metamorphic rocks: foliated (slate, phyllite, schist, gneiss) and non-foliated (marble, quartzite)
BC mineral resources: copper, gold, coal, natural gas, and industrial minerals
Ethical and environmental dimensions of mining in BC and beyond

areas.name: Geological History and the Rock Record
areas.raw: Earth's geological and biological history interpreted from rock strata and fossil evidence.
topics: Principles of stratigraphy: original horizontality, superposition, cross-cutting relationships, lateral continuity
Relative dating: using stratigraphic principles and index fossils
Absolute dating: radioactive decay, half-life, and radiometric dating methods (U-Pb, K-Ar, C-14)
The geologic time scale: eons, eras, periods, and major events
Fossil record: types of fossilization, index fossils, and palaeontological interpretation
Major events in Earth history: Hadean, Archean, Proterozoic, Paleozoic, Mesozoic, Cenozoic highlights
BC's geological history: terranes, ancient oceans, and recent glaciation

areas.name: Plate Tectonics and Earth's Interior
areas.raw: Plate tectonic theory explaining changes within Earth and to Earth's crust.
topics: Earth's internal structure: crust, mantle, outer core, inner core — evidence from seismology
Seismic waves: P-waves, S-waves, surface waves, and what they reveal about Earth's interior
Evidence for plate tectonics: paleomagnetism, seafloor spreading, hot spots, and GPS data
Plate boundaries: divergent (mid-ocean ridges, rift valleys), convergent (subduction zones, collisions), transform
Volcanic activity: types, distribution, and relationship to plate boundaries
Earthquakes: focus, epicentre, magnitude scales, and seismic hazard in BC
Tectonic reconstructions: Pangaea, plate motion through geological time

areas.name: Rock Deformation
areas.raw: Form, arrangement, and structure of rocks affected by three-dimensional forces over time.
topics: Stress and strain: types of stress (compression, tension, shear) and rock response
Rock plasticity: factors — temperature, pressure, time, and mineral composition
Folds: anticlines, synclines, plunging folds, and fold identification in the field
Faults: normal, reverse, thrust, and strike-slip; fault identification and displacement
Joints and fractures: columnar jointing, regional joint systems
Geologic mapping: reading and constructing geologic maps and cross-sections
BC structural geology: fold and thrust belts, the Rocky Mountains, and coastal fault systems

areas.name: Weathering, Erosion, and Surface Processes
areas.raw: Weathering and erosion processes continually reshaping landscapes.
topics: Mechanical weathering: frost action, exfoliation, root wedging, abrasion
Chemical weathering: hydrolysis, oxidation, carbonation, and differential weathering
Soil formation: parent material, climate, organisms, topography, and time
Erosion and transportation: rivers, wind, ice, and waves as agents of erosion and deposition
Glaciation: continental and alpine glaciers, glacial erosion and deposition, drumlins, moraines, erratics
Groundwater: aquifer types, porosity, permeability, water table, and contamination
Mass wasting: rockfalls, landslides, debris flows, and solifluction; risk in BC
First Peoples knowledge of landscape change and landforms in BC
